notes: Recording police in public is generally legal in Canada as long as you do not interfere with their duties or obstruct their work.
Home CCTV is generally legal in Canada, especially when used to monitor your own property. Privacy concerns arise if cameras capture neighbouring properties or public areas.
Employee monitoring is legal in Canada if it is reasonable, transparent, and necessary for business purposes. Excessive or hidden monitoring can breach privacy laws.
Canada generally follows a one-party consent rule, meaning you can record a conversation if you are part of it. Recording others without involvement is more restricted.
Recording video in public places is generally allowed in Canada, as long as it does not intrude on reasonable expectations of privacy or involve harassment.
Canada allows phone call recording under a one-party consent rule, meaning you can record if you are part of the call.
GPS tracking in Canada is lawful in limited situations, but secretly tracking another person without consent can violate criminal or privacy laws.
Drone use is legal in Canada under aviation rules, but using drones for surveillance can raise privacy concerns depending on how and where they are used.
Businesses in Canada can use CCTV for security, but they must respect privacy laws and ensure monitoring is reasonable and necessary.
Hidden cameras in Canada can be legal in limited contexts, but using them in places where people expect privacy is likely unlawful.
